name: Bellerophon
description: Bellerophon, a hero of Greek mythology, was famed for his courage and skill, particularly his ability to tame the winged horse Pegasus. Born in Corinth, his early life was marred by tragedy, including the accidental killing of his brother, which led to his exile. He sought purification from King Proetus of Tiryns, but the king's wife, Stheneboea (or Anteia), falsely accused Bellerophon of attempting to seduce her. Enraged, Proetus sent Bellerophon to King Iobates of Lycia with a sealed message instructing Iobates to kill him. However, bound by the laws of hospitality, Iobates instead tasked Bellerophon with a series of dangerous feats, hoping he would perish. Bellerophon successfully completed these tasks, including slaying the Chimera, a fire-breathing monster with the head of a lion, the body of a goat, and the tail of a serpent. He also fought against the Amazons and defeated Lycian pirates. Impressed by Bellerophon's prowess, Iobates revealed the contents of Proetus' letter and offered Bellerophon his daughter, Philonoe, in marriage, granting him half his kingdom. Bellerophon's hubris eventually led to his downfall. He attempted to fly Pegasus to Mount Olympus to join the gods, an act of excessive pride that angered Zeus. Zeus sent a gadfly to sting Pegasus, causing Bellerophon to fall back to Earth. He survived, but spent the rest of his life wandering alone, crippled and despised by the gods, a cautionary tale against hubris and overreaching ambition. His story highlights themes of exile, betrayal, divine favor, and the consequences of pride.
subtitle: Tamer of Pegasus, Slayer of Chimera
achievements: Marrying Philonoe and gaining a kingdom

keyMyths.title: The Taming of Pegasus
keyMyths.description: Bellerophon, guided by the seer Polyeidos, slept in the temple of Athena, who provided him with a golden bridle. He used this bridle to tame the winged horse Pegasus, allowing him to accomplish great feats.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of the Chimera
keyMyths.description: King Iobates tasked Bellerophon with killing the Chimera, a fearsome monster. Riding Pegasus, Bellerophon soared above the Chimera and eventually killed it, either by piercing it with his spear or by melting its throat with a lump of lead attached to his spear.

keyMyths.title: Bellerophon's Fall
keyMyths.description: Bellerophon, in his hubris, attempted to fly Pegasus to Mount Olympus. Zeus, angered by this arrogance, sent a gadfly to sting Pegasus, causing Bellerophon to fall to Earth, where he lived out his days as a wanderer.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides early accounts of Bellerophon's exploits, including the slaying of the Chimera.
sources.text: Provides early accounts of Bellerophon's exploits, including the slaying of the Chimera.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Mentions Pegasus and Bellerophon's connection to the winged horse.
sources.text: Mentions Pegasus and Bellerophon's connection to the winged horse.

sources.author: Pindar
sources.description: Details Bellerophon's attempt to reach Olympus and his subsequent fall.
sources.text: Details Bellerophon's attempt to reach Olympus and his subsequent fall.

associations.name: Pegasus
associations.significance: Represents Bellerophon's ambition and mastery over the natural world.

associations.name: Bridle of Athena
associations.significance: Symbolizes divine assistance and favor in achieving the impossible.

associations.name: Chimera
associations.significance: Represents the triumph of order and courage over chaos and monstrous threats.

associations.name: Courage
associations.significance: Highlights Bellerophon's bravery in facing formidable foes.

associations.name: Hubris
associations.significance: Illustrates the dangers of excessive pride and ambition leading to downfall.

associations.name: Lycia
associations.significance: Signifies the distant lands where Bellerophon proves his worth and faces trials.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both heroes slay a dragon/monster (Chimera/Fafnir) and achieve great renown, but face tragic ends.
Both heroes undertake seemingly impossible quests and demonstrate exceptional strength and courage.
Both receive divine assistance (Athena/Indra) to acquire powerful weapons or abilities to overcome their adversaries.

worshipPractices.name: Hero Shrines
worshipPractices.description: While not widely worshipped as a god, Bellerophon may have received localized veneration through hero shrines, where offerings and prayers were made to honor his achievements and seek his protection. These shrines would likely be found in Corinth or Lycia.

cultural.modernLegacy: Bellerophon's story continues to inspire modern interpretations in literature, film, and art, often serving as a cautionary tale about the perils of ambition and the importance of humility. His association with Pegasus makes him a symbol of inspiration and creativity.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Exile
extendedContent.content: Bellerophon, son of Glaucus (some accounts say Poseidon) and Eurynome, was born in Corinth. His early life was marked by misfortune when he accidentally killed his brother, or, in some versions of the myth, another Corinthian. This act of manslaughter forced him into exile. Seeking purification, Bellerophon traveled to Tiryns and presented himself to King Proetus. The king granted him sanctuary and purification, unaware of the troubles that would soon follow. Proetus' wife, Stheneboea (or Anteia), developed a passionate but unrequited love for the hero. Rejected by Bellerophon, she spitefully accused him of attempting to seduce her. Enraged by these false accusations, Proetus could not directly violate the laws of hospitality by killing Bellerophon himself. Instead, he devised a treacherous plan to be rid of him, sending him on a perilous mission to the court of King Iobates in Lycia, armed with a sealed letter containing instructions for Iobates to kill the bearer.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Triumphs
extendedContent.content: Upon arriving in Lycia, Bellerophon was initially welcomed by King Iobates, unaware of the letter's deadly contents. After several days of feasting and hospitality, Iobates finally read the letter and, bound by the same laws of hospitality as Proetus, hesitated to kill Bellerophon directly. Instead, he presented Bellerophon with a series of increasingly dangerous tasks, hoping he would perish in the attempt. The first and most famous of these tasks was to slay the Chimera, a monstrous fire-breathing hybrid with the head of a lion, the body of a goat, and the tail of a serpent. With the aid of Athena, who provided him with a golden bridle, Bellerophon captured and tamed the winged horse Pegasus. Mounting Pegasus, he soared into the sky and, using his aerial advantage, defeated the Chimera by either thrusting a lead-tipped spear into its mouth, the lead melting and suffocating the beast, or by simply showering it with arrows from above. Bellerophon also conquered the Solymi and the Amazons, proving his strength and courage in battle.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Downfall
extendedContent.content: Bellerophon's victories brought him great fame and honor. King Iobates, impressed by his valor, eventually revealed Proetus' letter and gave Bellerophon his daughter Philonoe in marriage, bestowing upon him half of his kingdom. Bellerophon became a Lycian hero, and his lineage continued through his children. However, his success bred hubris. In his pride, Bellerophon attempted to fly Pegasus to Mount Olympus, the home of the gods, believing himself worthy of joining them. Zeus, angered by this act of arrogance, sent a gadfly to sting Pegasus, causing the winged horse to buck Bellerophon off its back. Bellerophon fell back to Earth, crippled and blinded. He wandered the earth alone, despised by the gods and alienated from humanity, until his death. Bellerophon's story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of pride and the limits of human ambition, even for the most celebrated heroes.

shortDescription: Greek hero who tamed Pegasus and slew the Chimera, but fell from grace due to hubris. A symbol of courage and the perils of overweening pride.
